channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Global Oil Prices Rise as Conflict With Iran Deepens
item.description: The Houthis claimed to have attacked two ships in the Red Sea, and Iran said a third caught fire near the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Laura Stevens
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Diesel Crisis Puts Poor Farmers Around the World at Risk
item.description: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz has limited the shipment of diesel, depriving farmers of fuel for irrigation pumps and threatening the food supply.
item.dc:creator: Peter S. Goodman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Fabeha Monir for The New York Times

item.title: Clarity Act Mired In Debate Over Whether to Bar President From Selling Crypto
item.description: Democrats and Republicans are haggling about the Clarity Act, a major bill pushed by the crypto industry, as it moves closer to a Senate vote.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:description: President Trump disclosed in June that he and his family had made $1.4 billion in crypto revenue.

item.title: Three Airports Plan to Ditch T.S.A. Agents Amid Push for Private Security
item.description: The airports have opted in to the agency’s new security model, an agency spokesperson confirmed on Wednesday. A union official said the move could jeopardize public safety.
item.dc:creator: Ryley Ober
item.media:description: Tampa International is one of three U.S. airports opting in to a new program.

item.title: Tesla Profit Falls Even as Car Sales Rebound
item.description: The company is selling more cars, but the company’s profit was down because of price cuts and higher expenses.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:description: Car sales account for more than 70 percent of Tesla’s revenue, but investors are more focused on the company’s efforts to develop self-driving taxis and robots with humanlike form.

item.title: Sony Revives Cinerama Dome in Los Angeles as Box Office Sales Rebound
item.description: The iconic Cinerama Dome and adjacent 14-screen theater complex will reopen in 2028 after a renovation. The venue closed in 2020 because of the pandemic.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.category: Cinerama Dome (Los Angeles, Calif, Movie Theater)
item.media:credit: Kate Warren for The New York Times
item.media:description: The 956-seat Cinerama Dome in Hollywood first opened in 1963.

item.title: Trump’s Trade Negotiator Doubles Down on Tariffs Before Global Duties Expire
item.description: Jamieson Greer, the president’s top trade negotiator, told the Senate that the United States remained intent on tariffs as it prepared to replace duties the Supreme Court struck down.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.category: Office of the United States Trade Representative
item.media:description: At a Senate hearing on Wednesday, the U.S. trade representative, Jamieson Greer, said he considered the administration’s trade policy a success.

item.title: Stocks and the Economy Are Increasingly Relying on the A.I. Boom
item.description: Investment in artificial intelligence and related companies is lifting the stock market and spending across the economy.
item.dc:creator: Ben Casselman and Joe Rennison

item.title: Trump’s New Trade Fights (and Deals)
item.description: President Trump has resumed his approach of big threats against some trading partners and generous pacts with others. Experts see new risks.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Lauren McCarthy
item.media:description: Trade fights and agreements are increasingly front of mind for President Trump.

item.title: Global Oil Price Jumps as War Threats Intensify
item.description: But stock investors shrug off Iran developments as they await crucial earnings reports from tech firms including Alphabet and Tesla.
item.dc:creator: Laura Stevens and Jason Karaian
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tomas Munita for The New York Times

item.title: See How Houthis Put the Red Sea at Risk as an Alternative Oil Route
item.description: Saudi Arabia has diverted huge amounts of oil to the Red Sea since the Iran war began, but now the Iranian-backed Houthi militants say that they intend to block that route.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott, Lazaro Gamio and Samuel Granados
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Laredo’s Future Rides on Trump’s Next Trade Move
item.description: The economy of Laredo, Texas, has boomed under the president’s global tariffs. Now, his demands may threaten its success.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times

item.title: Donald Trump Jr.’s Investment Firm Posts Staggering Returns of 200%
item.description: The fledgling firm, run by the president’s oldest son and his partner Omeed Malik, has made investments in A.I. and defense technology that have tripled in value in just months.
item.dc:creator: Maureen Farrell
item.media:credit: Chandan Khanna/AFP, via Getty Images (Malik); Alex Brandon/AP (Trump)
item.media:description: Omeed Malik, left, and Donald Trump Jr.

item.title: Trump Plans to Impose Tariffs on Generic Drugs
item.description: The administration has repeatedly said it will impose steep pharmaceutical tariffs but has not followed through on them.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins
item.media:credit: Mette Lampcov for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said he plans to impose tariffs on generic medicines in an effort to bring pharmaceutical manufacturing back to the United States.

item.title: Trump Lifts Ban on Direct Flights to Lebanon
item.description: The prohibition had been in place since 1985, when it was imposed in response to Hezbollah’s hijacking of TWA Flight 847.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump with President Joseph Aoun of Lebanon at the White House on Tuesday.

item.title: Houthis Threaten Red Sea Blockade, Putting Oil Market at Greater Risk
item.description: With the Strait of Hormuz effectively closed, the militant group’s threats in the Red Sea jeopardize Saudi Arabia’s main workaround for oil exports.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross, Rebecca F. Elliott and Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A satellite image shows Bab al-Mandab Strait off the coast of Yemen in February.

item.title: Review Says Gates Foundation Did Not Participate in Epstein Crimes
item.description: A review by an outside law firm hired by the Gates Foundation investigated only the philanthropy and not Mr. Gates or his private office.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer
item.category: Wilmer Cutler Pickering Hale and Dorr LLP
item.media:description: Bill Gates, chairman of the Gates Foundation, at an event in New Delhi in 2025.

item.title: Oil Buyers Battered by the Iran War Energy Crisis Race to Build Buffers
item.description: As rising fuel costs clobber their economies, the Philippines and other developing countries in Asia are pushing to build strategic petroleum reserves to cushion against future shocks.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.media:credit: Jes Aznar for The New York Times
item.media:description: A road in Manila. After the start of the war in Iran, gasoline prices doubled and diesel prices tripled in the Philippines, setting off a sudden spike in inflation and transit strikes.

item.title: Trump Escalates Canada Tariffs as Mark Carney Holds Firm
item.description: The president’s new tariffs on a top U.S. trading partner aim at gaining concessions, but the Canadian government, and the public, have been bracing for the escalation.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: Steel coils at the Algoma Steel facility in Ontario in July. U.S. tariffs on goods such as steel, aluminum and vehicles have hit sensitive Canadian sectors.

item.title: Will the U.S. and China Build Walls Around A.I.?
item.description: Rapid advancements in Chinese artificial intelligence models reportedly have Washington and Beijing considering new ways to protect their technology sectors.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe, Ian Mount and Lauren Hirsch
item.category: Beijing Moonshot AI Technology Co Ltd
item.media:description: Some Trump administration officials are reportedly weighing new limits on Chinese artificial intelligence models.

item.title: Why States Are Making It Harder to Save Money With Solar Panels
item.description: The changes are costing homeowners money. But they also reflect a changing grid — and the need for home batteries.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Paris

item.title: Trump to Impose 50% Tariff on Many Canadian Goods
item.description: The administration will use an untested legal provision to put significant duties on Canadian exports, reigniting a clash with one of America’s biggest trading partners.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Ian Austen
item.media:description: The tariffs, which go into effect in 30 days, would be imposed on a range of Canadian exports, including wine and dairy products.

item.title: Andy Burnham, New UK Prime Minister, Names John Healey as Top Finance Official
item.description: As chancellor, John Healey, who quit as defense secretary in the former government, will fill one of the most consequential posts, overseeing fiscal and economic policy.
item.dc:creator: Eshe Nelson
item.media:description: John Healey on Monday. The former defense minister, he was named Britain’s top finance official.

item.title: Judge Temporarily Pauses Paramount-Warner Bros. Deal
item.description: The delay is intended to give the court time to consider a lawsuit that argues the $111 billion merger violates antitrust laws.
item.dc:creator: David McCabe
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Warner Bros. studio in Burbank, Calif.

item.title: Trump Administration Sought Phone Records of Times Journalists and Their Relatives
item.description: The government is aggressively seeking the confidential sources of Times reporters who wrote about the president’s new Air Force One. The Times has asked a judge to quash the subpoenas.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Lucia Vazquez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The New York Times building at 620 Eighth Avenue in Manhattan.

item.title: How Google’s A.I. Search Is Imperiling the Open Web
item.description: As Google incorporates more artificial intelligence into search, people are spending more time on Google. Some website operators are crying foul.
item.dc:creator: Kate Conger

item.title: Soaring Egg Prices Are Hitting China Hard
item.description: China consumes more eggs per capita than almost every other country, so a recent spike in costs is touching a nerve.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Qilai Shen for The New York Times

item.title: American A.I. Giants Like Alphabet Face Fresh Tests
item.description: Rapid advancements in Chinese artificial intelligence models raise more questions about costly technology spending, as Google’s parent prepares to report earnings.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.category: Beijing Moonshot AI Technology Co Ltd
item.media:description: Alibaba’s announcement of rapid advances in its latest artificial intelligence model add to pressure on Western technology giants.

item.title: U.S. Average Gas Price Returns to $4 a Gallon as Iran Crisis Escalates
item.description: A month ago, drivers were enjoying some relief at the pump after the U.S. and Iran signed a deal intended to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Now, prices are rising again.
item.dc:creator: Katie Robertson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gasoline prices, which had been falling in recent weeks, are almost back up to where they were a month ago.

item.title: Oil Markets on Edge as Houthi Rebels Threaten Shipping in the Middle East
item.description: The global oil price went above $90 but pulled back on signs of hope for a reduction in tensions.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: ‘The Odyssey’ Brings in $124.5 Million at the Box Office, Powered by Christopher Nolan
item.description: Christopher Nolan’s nearly three-hour epic was No. 1 over the weekend, with about $124.5 million in ticket sales in the United States and Canada.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes

item.title: The Case of the 3:30 Emails
item.description: Plus: Are you really paying attention to me on Zoom?
item.dc:creator: Max Read
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Margeaux Walter for The New York Times

item.title: Step Into the ‘Zone of Genius’ (Before A.I. Takes Your Job)
item.description: A decades-old self-help concept is gaining new purchase among people searching for meaningful work in the age of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Lora Kelley

item.title: The ‘Bad Blood’ Between Polymarket’s Shayne Coplan and Kalshi’s Tarek Mansour
item.description: Shayne Coplan of Polymarket and Tarek Mansour of Kalshi are young billionaires intent on dominating prediction markets. It’s more than a battle. It’s “bad blood.”
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany and William K. Rashbaum

item.title: Adidas Hopes Its World Cup Moment Can Drive U.S. Sales
item.description: Nearly everything on the field on Sunday will bear the German company’s logo. Bjorn Gulden, the chief executive, says he “couldn’t have scripted it better.”
item.dc:creator: Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bjorn Gulden, the Adidas chief executive. The World Cup has served as a springboard for the German company’s ambitions in the United States.

item.title: Madison Square Garden Sues Wired Magazine Over L.G.B.T.Q. Tracking Report
item.description: The Manhattan venue claims a July article from the technology magazine falsely implied that it tracked information about some celebrities’ sexual orientation for “discriminatory purposes.”
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald and Mack Liederman
item.media:credit: Jutharat Pinyodoonyachet for The New York Times
item.media:description: Wired reported in a July 9 article that the venue maintained a talent database containing information about the gender identity and sexual orientation of some V.I.P. guests.

item.title: America Caught World Cup Fever. His Job Is to Capitalize on It.
item.description: Don Garber, the M.L.S. commissioner, has been planning for this moment for eight years.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kessler

item.title: World Cup Final Drives Billions in Bets on Kalshi and Polymarket
item.description: The soccer tournament has helped propel the prediction markets to new user records, creating what may be the largest gambling event in history.
item.dc:creator: Lauren McCarthy

item.title: Robinhood’s Chief on Trump Accounts and Why Trading Isn’t Necessarily Gambling
item.description: Vlad Tenev, the chief executive of the digital brokerage Robinhood, is trying to win over the next generation of Americans with Trump accounts.
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: Aaron Wojack for The New York Times
item.media:description: Vlad Tenev, who founded Robinhood in 2013, sees the company’s partnership with the government on Trump accounts as a way to expand financial access more broadly.

item.title: Russia Bill Broadens Sanctions but Could Fuel Fears Over the Dollar
item.description: The Trump administration has recently sought to scale back sanctions as adversaries develop workarounds that some believe could erode the U.S. dollar’s supremacy.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Office of Foreign Assets Control (United States Treasury)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Treasury Department in Washington.

item.title: What Will You Do in Retirement? Your Money Depends on It.
item.description: Workers often focus solely on their retirement savings balance. But figuring out how you want to spend the rest of your life could be job No. 1.
item.dc:creator: Brian J. O’Connor
item.media:credit: Benjamin Cleeton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Before Liz Klohmann retired, a leadership coach urged her to pursue “your Liz 2.0.”

item.title: Why Locals Are Gatekeeping Their Favorite Places From Tourists
item.description: As visitors flock to places like Mexico City, Milan and Copenhagen, residents are rethinking what they share, and whom they share it with.
item.dc:creator: Tariro Mzezewa
item.media:credit: Mads Claus Rasmussen/Ritzau Scanpix, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Copenhagen, one of Europe’s fastest growing destinations, is among the places where some residents refuse to share the names of their favorite parks and beaches with visitors. Above, a group of tourists gaze at the Opera House.

item.title: Cyclospora Investigation Turns to Farms in Mexico
item.description: The F.D.A. is planning to inspect farms and a lettuce shredding facility to determine how lettuce supplied by the produce company Taylor Farms to Taco Bell became contaminated and where it was shipped.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Dani Blum
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Andrew Mangum for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Food and Drug Administration’s investigation is focusing on the source of the contamination by the cyclospora parasite.

item.title: Taylor Farms to Remove Products After Lettuce Is Linked to Cyclospora Cases
item.description: The California company grows produce in more than 12 states, and in Mexico and Canada. Taco Bell and food distributor Sysco have pulled Taylor Farms lettuce from supply chains.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell, Christina Jewett and Kevin Draper

item.title: China’s Moonshot AI Unveils Kimi Model, Threatening America’s Lead
item.description: China’s Moonshot AI unveiled a freely available artificial intelligence model that seemed to narrow the gap with cutting-edge offerings from U.S. tech companies.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Cade Metz
item.category: Beijing Moonshot AI Technology Co Ltd
item.media:description: The Moonshot AI booth at the World Artificial Intelligence Conference on Friday in Shanghai. The company’s Kimi K3 model performed as well at some tasks as the leading models from U.S. firms.

item.title: X and Music Publishers Retreat From Lawsuits
item.description: Elon Musk’s social media platform and the music companies agreed to dismiss dueling claims, from 2023 and 2026. They have not said why.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: Elon Musk’s social media company did not respond to requests for comment on the agreement, which was made public on Friday.

item.title: After Repeated Crises, Boeing Looks to Turn a Corner
item.description: The company recently opened a new production line to keep up with strong demand for its 737 Max, though orders for its new planes lag rival Airbus.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi

item.title: Pentagon’s Escort Policy for Journalists Is Temporarily Restored
item.description: An appeals court said the Defense Department could require escorts for reporters who visit the Pentagon while The New York Times sues to overturn the rule.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Jared Soares for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Pentagon is seen from the Washington Monument. One judge on a three-judge panel wrote that he would have allowed the pause in policy to remain in effect.
